linux: an open-source operating system kernel used by many distributions
gnu: a free software project providing essential tools for Linux systems
windows: a proprietary operating system developed by Microsoft
macos: a proprietary operating system developed by Apple
android: a mobile operating system developed by Google
freebsd: a free and open-source Unix-like operating system
haiku: an open-source operating system inspired by BeOS
solaris: a Unix operating system developed by Sun Microsystems
ios: a mobile operating system developed by Apple for iPhone
chromeos: a Linux-based operating system developed by Google for Chromebooks
opensuse: a stable Linux distribution sponsored by SUSE
nixos: a Linux distribution built around the Nix package manager
pop os: a Linux distribution developed by System76
kali: a Debian-based distribution focused on penetration testing
raspbian: a Debian-based distribution optimized for Raspberry Pi
slackware: one of the oldest actively maintained Linux distributions
centos: a community-driven Red Hat Enterprise Linux clone
rhel: Red Hat Enterprise Linux, a commercial Linux distribution
arch: a lightweight and flexible Linux distribution
debian: a stable Linux distribution known for its package management system
ubuntu: a popular Linux distribution based on Debian
void linux: a rolling release Linux distribution using runit
gentoo: a source-based Linux distribution for advanced users
alpine: a minimal Linux distribution designed for security and simplicity
paru: an AUR helper and pacman wrapper written in Rust
snap: installs sandboxed packages developed by Canonical
nix: package manager for NixOS and other systems
yum: legacy package manager for Red Hat-based systems
cargo: Rust package manager and build tool
stat: displays detailed file or filesystem status
file: determines the type of a file
wc: counts lines, words, and characters in a file
ls: lists files in your current directory
mv: moves or renames files or directories
fd: a simple and fast find alternative
lsof: lists open files and the processes using them
lsblk: lists information about all available block devices
bash: the default shell on most Linux distributions
zsh: a powerful shell with improved features over bash
ksh: KornShell, a Unix shell with scripting features
dash: a minimal POSIX-compliant shell used for scripts
uptime: shows how long the system has been running
kernel: the core of the operating system, managing hardware and system processes
strace: traces system calls and signals made by a program
sleep: pauses execution for a set time
sudo: runs a command with superuser privileges
systemctl: controls and manages systemd services and units
source: executes a file in the current shell session
which: shows the full path of a command
type: describes how a command would be interpreted
jobs: lists background jobs in the current session
bg: resumes a suspended job in the background
fg: brings a background job to the foreground
alias: creates a shortcut for a command
neovim: a modern fork of vim with better extensibility
vimtutor: a tutorial program for learning vim
man: displays the manual for a command
info: displays extended information about a command
apropos: searches the manual page names and descriptions
traceroute: traces the path packets take to a host
curl: transfers data from or to a server
ssh: connects to a remote machine securely
sway: a tiling window manager compatible with i3 configuration
i3: a tiling window manager for X11
bspwm: a tiling window manager for X11
git: version control system for tracking code changes
git diff: shows changes between commits or working tree
adb: Android Debug Bridge for controlling devices
adb install: installs an APK on a device
adb uninstall: removes an app from a device
adb logcat: displays system logs from the device
adb shell: opens a shell on the device
fastboot: tool for flashing partitions on Android devices
microsoft: an American technology company known for Windows and Azure
apple: an American technology company known for macOS, iOS, and hardware products
fortune: prints a random quote or joke
ghost: apparently there's a ghost in your terminal
matrix: displays a scrolling green code effect
foo: placeholder name often used in examples
bar: another placeholder often paired with foo
qux: the final placeholder of the quartet
